speaker: interviewer
text: Tape five-- the date is June 30th, 1995. We're doing an interview with Judith Goldstein, born Shapiro. My name is Alice Braun. This is New Rochelle, New York. And the language spoken is English. What happened-- when did you leave this DP camp where you were located, and you went to school and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You stayed--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually, I, I finished together with the rest of them, what they called a matura. It was a, a graduation that included more than high school. It was like a lyceum. It was two years extra. And by that time, I had already completed several years of my music educational. Also, I attended the conservatory of music in Offenbach. It's right outside of Frankfurt am Main. And that was, of course-- I had a piano. It was a great experience for me. I resumed, really, acquainting myself with not just the piano but also with the literature of music, about composers, many composers, including Wagner, which was really-- I had mixed feelings about that. But I, I liked his music, I have to say, even though I, I knew exactly what his, his activities were.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had a pretty good repertoire of knowledge at that point about music, about composers, their style of, of compositions, et cetera. We got to Germany by many routes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I'm sorry. I-- this was already Germany. Yes. That's all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- when did, when did your mother and you decide to leave Germany, and your brother, of course?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we, we were going to go to Israel. But my mother did contact her relatives in United States. And the goal was, at that point, going to the United States, while most of my friends went to Israel. We stayed on longer in Sulzheim until we got a quota. It was not easy. The Polish quota was not moving at all. But finally, we did get on the quota. And in 1949, I came to the States. At that point, I already knew my husband, because I came in to fix my teeth in Germany. And even though he wasn't a full-fledged dentist, he was working in the mouth and going to school. And he actually did my first cap or something. And that's where we met.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you came to New York, you--

speaker: interviewee
text: He met me at the boat. He was here already. Actually, I didn't know if I was ever going to see him again, because my goal was to go to Israel. And he was in the States already. But we did meet a year later. And it didn't take too long for us to get married. He was alone. Even though I was very young, he was alone. He, he has a sister, but she was married. And he was alone and lonely. And we were in love. So we decided to get married.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1950. It was a year later. We got married in March 25th. And we've been married happily ever since. We have two children. Our son's name is Leslie. He calls himself Ben-Zvi, the son of Harry. He has a law degree. And he, he just didn't want to be called in a Germanic name. So he, he, he took a Hebrew name, the son of Harry. He's really registered as Leslie Chaim Ben-Zvi. He's named after my father. And our daughter, Renee, is-- was born after. [LAUGHS] You know, she's a few years younger. Leslie is actually 39. He's going to be 40. And Renee's 35. And my son, unfortunately, with all his successes, is getting a divorce. And he does have a little boy, Lee Am, Lee Am, my nation, Lee Am, Lee Am Ben-Zvi. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is this little boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's four and a half. And my daughter's children, you will see them in a minute. Today is my little grandson's birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, happy birthday.

speaker: interviewee
text: June 30th-- he's eight years old today. And I'm very proud of him, because he sings in Hebrew, in Yiddish, in Russian, and in French. He likes to do things with me together.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Is he musically inclined like yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- yes, but I think more in the arts.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: He really loves to paint, you know? And my daughter is an x-ray technician. She does anything that deals with x-ray and works in a hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you have a nice family, according to this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the children-- how many children does your daughter have? Two?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter has two. And my son has one. And my husband, Harry, of course was here to support me and, and help me through my schooling. It was not--

speaker: interviewer
text: You went gone back to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not until my children were-- I think Renee was about eight, my daughter-- that I felt that I could take the time to go back to school. Education was very important to me. And I had lost so much. So I went to Manhattanville College. And they actually gave me credit, two years. And I-- within a short period of time, I, I got my bachelor's. And then I went on to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Bachelor's in what?

speaker: interviewee
text: In arts.

speaker: interviewer
text: Arts.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I went on to study at Columbia University. It was actually a joint program between Herbert Lehman College and Columbia University in music. And so I have a master's in music. And for a while, I was teaching at the Larchmont Chatsworth School. And I worked with learning and developmentally disabled. But it, it started first with the special ed classes, when they came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I found that music, as well as art, is, is like almost a tool for communication, especially when you reach the difficult child, who has difficulty communicating verbally. And I began to use music as a tool for communication. And the principal once called me. And he said, did you ever consider music therapy and, and-- or art therapy?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I, I said, no. I mean, it never even occurred to me. He says, because I like what you're doing with the kids. You're doing creative movement. You help them-- you have them use the body as a tool.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I gave it some serious thought. And I enrolled in a program at Columbia Presbyterian Medical Center. It was a two-and-a-half-year program, where I learned to deal with really neurologically-impaired children, severely, really impaired children, was in the category of disabled children rather than, rather than development-- learning disabled. And this is what you made a career--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- this is what-- I made a career out of this, yes. And of course, not to ignore my arts, I, I studied art. My minors were art. I spent all my electives on art and participated in many art exhibits. And it wasn't until about 10 years ago, maybe a little bit more, that I started to express my memories through the arts. And I started first with the visual arts. And then it went on to composing music as well. Because I knew how to write music at this point and orchestrate.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I started to do a lot of artwork. Some are sad. And some are, are happy. Those, of course, that depict my, my experiences during the war are with great sorrow. Those that are, are the happier part of me are a lot in dance. My music comes through quite a lot through the happier paintings. And a lot of them are in dance. Will you show us some of those?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'll be very happy to show you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we'll be, we'll be looking at your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Artwork as well as your musical piece that you will probably play, play for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in January, I opened up in the Minnesota Museum of Art. And I also performed my pieces at the opening. And this was-- my exhibit was on there for five months. And they put my work on the cover of the magazine. Of the-- actually, it's not a magazine. It's a, it's a catalog, which will be placed in various museums, including a museum in, in-- the Holocaust Museum in Washington. And the curriculum-- the, the St. Paul and Minneapolis school system got together and did a curriculum. Again, they chose another piece of my work to put on the cover. And it's now being taught in the school system.

speaker: interviewer
text: So which one will you play for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I will probably play for you the pieces that I performed at the museum at Minneapolis in the same-- in the Minnesota Museum of Art, three compositions that are original compositions based on the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the Holocaust. Thank you so much. And we will continue with the musical part of this interview in one-- a few moments. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Smile.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi. This is my family, my husband, Harry, my daughter, Renee, my grandson, my first grandson, Matthew, and my granddaughter, Jillian. And today's Matthew's birthday. Today, he's eight years old. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We're a happy family. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Renee's husband is missing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My son is, is not available today. His name is Leslie Ben-Zvi. And he has a little boy called Lee Am, too. The name of the song is 'To Forgive and Forget.' Its music and lyrics by Judith Goldstein, me. And it basically deals with my memories and unresolved issues in my life about the war. Even though I tried very hard to come to peace, it's not easy. And what I'm trying to do is express myself through the arts. And this is one of the songs that I had written. [MUSIC - JUDITH GOLDSTEIN, 'TO FORGIVE AND FORGET']

speaker: interviewer
text: (SINGING) To forgive and forget, I was taught. That was my original thought. To bury my past and put it to rest, that would be best. I begged and begged, let me be aware that a tone was for me. Can't you see? Can't you see? Let me be. Let me be. Set me free.

speaker: interviewee
text: The sorrow of my childhood was on my mind, wanting so to leave it behind. Tormented by memories, the nights were scary. The days were grim. I wanted to scream. Though hard to bear, I needed to share my pain and tell and cry, reveal and heal and seal the past at last, at last. To forgive and forget I was taught. That was my original thought. Now I tell the world that a tunnel of hell for me will never be. Now I tell the world that a tunnel of hell for me will never be, will never be. Excuse me. Another song-- it's called, 'This Earth is Here for You and Me.' And it really deals with my anger and dismay about what still goes on in the world. It's like the world has not learned about anything. So this song deals a little bit about my past. And it also includes my dismay about what's happening in today with wars. 'This Earth is Here for You and Me.' [MUSIC - JUDITH GOLDSTEIN, 'THIS EARTH IS HERE FOR YOU AND ME']

words.text: [MUSIC - JUDITH GOLDSTEIN, 'THIS EARTH IS HERE FOR YOU AND ME']

speaker: interviewer
text: (SINGING) I love this planet Earth. Does it love me, too? It gave me much and also took away. What happened long ago, it seems that nothing's changed. Evils still appear in many shapes. Earth, please remember, sounds of the young still echoes laughter followed by cries.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, please, I beg you now, stop this mean display. I cannot bear to see that children have to be afraid to use the streets to gather, sing, and play. It looks like the reflection of my childhood is to stay. Don't close the iron gate before it is too late. This Earth is here for you and me. I know we can survive. We must change our lives and teach our children love, not hate, and teach our children love, not hat, and teach our children love, not hate, and teach our children love, not hate. These are my grandparents. And it's my father's-- I'm sorry, it's my mother's father and my mother's mother. And her name was Feyga. I think his name was Shimon. I'm not sure. And they lived in Valozhyn, in a small town in Poland. And of course, they perished before the war. These are my parents, probably taken before I was born and probably before my mother-- before my brother was born, even though I do remember them, especially my father, looking the way he looked. My mother did survive the war. So I did see her growing old. But I never saw my father grow old. He was only 38. This is my parents and myself and my brother. This is the only photo I have of me as a little girl. I don't think I was more than two and a half there. And we obtained this picture coming to the United States. It was at our relatives' homes, because all of our pictures were destroyed. And it was-- I was happy to see some pictures of myself and my parents, including my brother. This picture was taken in Sulzheim after the war. Maybe it was 1948. I'm not quite sure. And it was of the three of us-- my mother, my brother, and myself. We lived in sort of a kibbutz setting. And we decided that it's time to have a picture of the three of us. This is my wedding picture. And we got married in 1950 in New York, March 25th. And it looks more glamorous than it was. I was-- this was a borrowed gown. And I loved it. And I was just happy to marry my handsome Harry. This is my daughter, Renee, my son-in-law, Joseph. And their-- this is their wedding picture. And it was just a very happy day of my life. And they're having-- they have two children, Renee-- Jillian and Matthew. And I'm just happy to have been at the wedding. This is my son, Leslie Chaim Ben-Zvi. He is named after my father. And this is him today with his little boy, Lee Am, Lee Am, my nation. And I'm very proud of him. He's also a musician. And we share a lot of things together. We have a lot of concerts together. He's a fine pianist. And he's also a fine guitarist. And we love doing Hebrew songs together. These are my grandchildren, Matthew, in the center, who's eight today, and Jillian on his left, actually on his right, who is five. Her name is Jillian. She is named after my mother, Jillian. And her Hebrew name is [? Jael. ?] And I love that name. And Lee Am is on the left of Matthew. And his name is Lee Am, my nation. This is an original artwork done by me based on the ring that my father made me. It's the logo of the Vilna Ghetto, the triangle on the right side, the V, the two lines-- the, the [? vov ?] and the [? gimmel ?] on the left, which stands for Vilna Ghetto. And in this artwork, I have included my memories of the Vilna Ghetto. And if you notice on the very point on the top, I have people holding each other in arm. No matter what, we stand together. This painting is currently on exhibit in the Minnesota Museum of Art and is going to be traveling all over the United States. It's going to Columbus Museum very soon and will be traveling all over the United States. We'll run upstairs and get-- This is called 'The Last Journey.' This, too, is at the museum. And it's part of the collection that will be traveling the United States. It deals with my memories of the concentration camp and ghetto, watching the wagon filled with bodies and the person that pulls it. And on top, I try to depict the, the iron hand, like almost the devil himself, and the vultures, kind of waiting. It was not easy for me to do this piece. But it definitely deals with my memories of the concentration camp. This painting is based on Ponary, which I discussed in my testimony about. This is the place where at least 50,000 Jews were slaughtered. And now, it's a park. And the grass is green. And the flowers are in bloom. And the trees are in bloom. And the birds are singing. When I think of that place, I can't see real foliage growing there. I did this painting. And I see other things growing. I see limbs and people growing out of the earth, which is soaked with Jewish blood. This artwork is called 'Behind the Fence.' And again, this is an expression of my times in concentration camp. Even though I did not use a female image, I do remember the prayers that took place in-- behind a fence. And the prayers were, of course, to God, if he heard us. [INAUDIBLE] [BACKROUND NOISE] This is a recent artwork. And it deals with what we call the Final Solution. I am trying to record in every section, a different happening. Each section stands on its own. And basically, what I'm trying to do here is record history and talk about the various atrocities that took place in the ghettos, in Warsaw, in Buchenwald, in Bergen-Belsen, in Treblinka, in Babi Yar, in Chelmno, and of course, the Vilna Ghetto, and the barracks, and the shooting, and the crematory on the left lower corner, and the mockery of the Germans toward the Jews, saying all kinds of nasty things, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And it was not easy to do. I think each section stands on its own. This is also a recent work that I did, showing a particular face of horror. And it, it includes the eyebrow, as we call it, the eyebrow. It includes the march of people, children and women, going toward death, turning into skeletons on the upper-right corner. And their final step, of course, is death. The hat is made out of bones after the crematory. And I put in a Yizkor candle. Plus, I have little tiny names of like, Rivka, Leah, Avraham, names of people who did exist. And in the eyes, I am showing the horror of tears and fire.
